food_name: Bak Chang (Hokkien-style Rice Dumpling with Braised Pork and Salted Egg)
aliases: Bak Chang (Hokkien-style Rice Dumpling with Braised Pork and Salted Egg)

english: Bak Chang (Hokkien-style Rice Dumpling with Braised Pork and Salted Egg)

region_of_origin: Fujian (Hokkien), China — Dragon Boat Festival staple, sold year-round in Singapore
food_subgroup: Glutinous rice parcels in bamboo leaves with braised pork belly, salted egg yolk, and dried shrimp

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Bak Chang. Closest parent is a steamed glutinous rice parcel in leaves with meat filling. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid code fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Glutinous rice parcel in bamboo leaves with braised pork belly, salted egg yolk, dried shrimp, and chestnut
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A00PB is the closest FoodEx2 parent for rice-based composite dishes; no exact code exists for Hokkien bak chang.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for bak chang or glutinous rice dumpling as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Hokkien bak chang weighs approximately 220g of edible contents (bamboo leaf wrapper excluded), delivering 517 kcal, 67.1g carbohydrate, and 1507mg sodium per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Bak Chang / Rice Dumpling (Hokkien pork) — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Bak Chang / Rice Dumpling (Hokkien pork). All per_100g values are taken from this verified entry. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — glutinous rice analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice (GI ~90–98)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hokkien bak chang as a composite dish. GI of 92 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice (GI range 90–98; representative value 92). The pork belly fat and dried shrimp filling provide modest GI moderation but cannot meaningfully offset the dominant glutinous rice base. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (92 × 67.1) / 100 = 61.7, rounded to 61.5 per input record. Assessment type: referenced analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content (685mg/100g; 1507mg per 220g serving) from dark soy sauce, dried shrimp, and salted duck egg yolk
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid bak chang or strictly limit intake to half a dumpling (~110g, ~754mg sodium) on festival occasions only, as a single standard serving exceeds the entire recommended daily sodium maximum of 2000mg WHO / 1500mg AHA for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycaemic load per serving (GL 61.5) driven by glutinous rice base (GI ~92) delivering 67.1g carbohydrate per standard 220g dumpling
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should avoid bak chang or limit intake to no more than one-quarter of a dumpling (~55g, GL ~15) per occasion, as one standard bak chang delivers a glycaemic load equivalent to approximately 2.5 cups of steamed jasmine rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined cardiovascular risk from high sodium (1507mg/serving), saturated fat (7.7g/serving), cholesterol (194mg/serving), and trans fat (0.22g/serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit bak chang to small festival portions only, as the combination of high sodium, saturated fat from pork belly, and cholesterol from salted egg yolk presents a significant cumulative cardiovascular burden in a single serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Proinflammatory profile: very high GI (rapid glucose spike), moderate saturated fat (3.5g/100g), and high sodium (685mg/100g) all promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit bak chang to small portions on festival occasions; the shiitake mushroom filling provides anti-inflammatory beta-glucans but cannot offset the proinflammatory impact of the glutinous rice base, pork belly fat, and high sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bak Chang (肉粽, Hokkien-style Rice Dumpling) is the quintessential Dragon Boat Festival food in Singapore — pyramid-shaped parcels of savoury glutinous rice filled with braised pork belly, salted duck egg yolk, dried shrimp, chestnut, and dried shiitake mushroom, all wrapped in dried bamboo leaves and boiled for 4–6 hours. The Hokkien Singapore version is distinguished by its dark, intensely savoury, five-spice-fragrant rice (pre-treated with dark soy sauce) surrounding a rich, oil-glossy filling. The bamboo leaves impart a distinctive herbal aroma. Each standard dumpling weighs approximately 220g of edible contents and delivers 517 kcal, 67.1g carbohydrate, 21.6g fat, 18.7g protein, and 1507mg sodium. Nutritionally, bak chang is one of the most clinically challenging traditional festival foods: it has an extremely high glycaemic load (GL 61.5 per serving) from glutinous rice (GI ~92), very high sodium from dark soy sauce, dried shrimp, and salted egg yolk, moderate saturated fat from pork belly, and significant cholesterol from salted egg yolk. Bak chang is sold year-round at Singapore hawker centres but consumption peaks dramatically during the Dragon Boat Festival (端午节, 5th month of the Chinese lunar calendar). It is dairy-free but contains crustacean (dried shrimp), egg, and soy allergens. Not suitable for patients with diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in standard serving sizes.
common_user_queries: how many calories in a rice dumpling

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes, with exact match to Bak Chang / Rice Dumpling (Hokkien pork). All core macros and secondary nutrients are verified. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for glutinous rice, which is the dominant ingredient and well-characterised in the GI literature.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Hokkien bak chang is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this composite dish. All other nutrient fields are populated from the verified HPB source. GI of 92 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice (GI 90–98) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for composite Hokkien bak chang exists in the published literature. GL per serving = (92 × 67.1) / 100 = 61.7, consistent with input record value of 61.5 (minor rounding difference accepted). trans_fat_g of 0.1g/100g is a low but non-zero value reported by HPB; retained as reported. Vitamin A reported as 28.0 mcg RAE per HPB source. Folate reported as 10.0 mcg DFE per HPB source. Selenium reported as 18.0 mcg per HPB source.
